evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-JSON translation for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult; handler classes contain no inline serialisation logic for those types.
GrammarConfigLoader, GrammarConfig, and GrammarConfigSerializer are three distinct, purpose-focused classes: file-IO in the loader, data in the value object, JSON rendering in the serialiser.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single error-propagation boundary for every HTTP route: IllegalArgumentException → 400, RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, IOException → 500.
SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a stable facade for the HTTP layer, delegating multi-seed and comparison operations to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator respectively.
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias centralises alias resolution in one place; ExplorationHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers both pass through it without duplicating alias logic.

ExplorationParams.collocatesPerSeed maps the HTTP parameter 'nouns_per' to a Java field with a different name; it is then re-emitted as 'nouns_per' in the response via an extraParams map. The two renames happen without a comment at either translation point.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples assembles its response with an inline HashMap + manual loop, while the sibling ExplorationHandlers delegates to ExploreResponseAssembler. The asymmetry is unexplained.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both manually destructure the same ExplorationParams record into slightly different options objects (SingleSeedExplorationOptions vs ExplorationOptions) inline inside the handler, producing thin ad-hoc glue code at the HTTP-to-domain seam.

resolveRelationConfig terminates with 'return relationConfig.orElseThrow()' after an explicit isEmpty() guard two lines above — the orElseThrow is unreachable and adds noise at the boundary validation seam.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler isolates all model-to-presentation translation in one place; neither ExplorationResult nor the handler classes carry JSON concerns.
The GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfigSerializer trio cleanly separates data, I/O, and serialisation into three focused collaborators with no back-and-forth coupling.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, predictable error-propagation contract across every HTTP route, eliminating per-handler try/catch scatter.
SemanticFieldExplorer correctly acts as a stable facade: the HTTP layer calls one class regardless of which underlying algorithm (single-seed, multi-seed, comparison) is invoked.
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias ensures alias-to-canonical-ID translation happens at exactly one seam, keeping both calling handlers free of alias logic.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The seam issues are concentrated at the HTTP-to-domain parameter-translation layer: minor naming discontinuities, thin inline glue code that bypasses the established assembler pattern, and a redundant guard that adds noise without protection. None cross into architectural problems, but they represent localised glue-code entropy at the input/output boundary of ExplorationHandlers.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates above-average mid-level elegance: dedicated translation objects, a clean facade, and uniform error propagation are all present and working. What pulls the score below 'solid' is a cluster of small but real seam imperfections in ExplorationHandlers: a parameter that is silently renamed twice as it crosses the HTTP boundary, inline options construction that duplicates the same field-extraction pattern in two sibling methods, and asymmetric response-assembly compared to the concordance handler. These are not deep structural failures, but they represent the kind of glue-code entropy the dimension rubric flags. Anchoring against the global scale: this is comfortably above 60 (no significant drag) but short of 85 (seam translation is uneven in one subsystem).

issues.summary: HTTP parameter 'nouns_per' is silently renamed to 'collocatesPerSeed' in ExplorationParams and then back to 'nouns_per' in the response, with no comment at either translation point
evidence: parseExplorationParams: 'int collocatesPerSeed = HttpApiUtils.parseIntParam(params, "nouns_per", 30);' — field name diverges silently from the HTTP parameter name.

handleSemanticFieldExplore: 'extraParams.put("nouns_per", exploreParams.collocatesPerSeed());' — the HTTP name is recreated manually for the response, meaning callers and readers must trace two renames to verify round-trip consistency.
No comment at either translation explains why the rename exists or which name is canonical.
issues.suggestion: Either align the ExplorationParams field name with the HTTP parameter ('nounsPerSeed' or keeping 'nouns_per' as the field name to match the wire name), or add a single comment at the ExplorationParams record explaining the deliberate rename and the canonical name used in responses. Centralising the key constant would also prevent the two sites diverging.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers manually destructures ExplorationParams into domain options objects inline in two sibling methods, producing thin ad-hoc glue code at the HTTP-to-domain seam
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: 'new SingleSeedExplorationOptions(new ExplorationOptions(exploreParams.topCollocates(), exploreParams.minLogDice(), exploreParams.minShared()), exploreParams.collocatesPerSeed())' — three fields unpacked inline.
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: 'new ExplorationOptions(exploreParams.topCollocates(), exploreParams.minLogDice(), exploreParams.minShared())' — same three fields unpacked again.
handleSemanticFieldComparison: 'new ExplorationOptions(exploreParams.topCollocates(), exploreParams.minLogDice(), exploreParams.minShared())' — identical inline construction a third time.
The pattern is repeated three times with no extraction into a helper, meaning a field addition or rename in ExplorationParams requires updating three call sites.
issues.suggestion: Add a 'toExplorationOptions()' factory method to ExplorationParams (or a package-private static helper) that constructs an ExplorationOptions from the record, and a 'toSingleSeedOptions()' variant for the single-seed case. All three handler methods then call the helper, centralising the translation in one place.

issues.summary: ConcordanceHandlers assembles its response with inline HashMap construction while sibling ExplorationHandlers delegates to ExploreResponseAssembler, leaving the pattern inconsistent across the API layer
evidence: ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples builds the 'examples' list inline with a manual HashMap loop: 'for (QueryResults.CollocateResult r : results) { Map<String, Object> exMap = new HashMap<>(); exMap.put("sentence", r.sentence()); ... }'.
ExplorationHandlers always delegates list building to ExploreResponseAssembler methods (populateExploreResponse, populateComparisonResponse, formatSeedCollocates, etc.).

The asymmetry means a developer adding a new field to concordance responses edits the handler directly, while for exploration responses they edit the assembler — different locations for the same kind of change.
issues.suggestion: Extract concordance response assembly into ExploreResponseAssembler (or a sibling ConcordanceResponseAssembler) so the handler class contains only HTTP-layer logic. The concordance examples list formatting is simple enough that a static helper method is sufficient.

root_causes: ExplorationHandlers grew to own both HTTP parsing and domain-options construction without a dedicated parameter-to-options translation object, creating repeated inline glue at the input boundary.
ConcordanceHandlers was extracted from a larger class after ExploreResponseAssembler was already established, so it did not inherit the assembler pattern.
likely_symptoms: nouns_per_rename_seam — the silent rename is a symptom of the HTTP parameter vocabulary not being aligned with the domain options vocabulary.
inline_options_construction_glue — the repeated destructuring is a symptom of ExplorationParams having no built-in translation to domain types.
